ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper brings a moderately novel combination: it applies variational free‑energy/MLE asymptotic Gaussian posterior ideas (Sec.3) to pool‑based batch active learning for deep CNNs and makes the approach scalable via Kronecker‑factored (KFAC) approximations of the Fisher (Sec.3.2) and further heuristic approximations to obtain a greedy selection criterion. However the individual ingredients are established (Graves 2011 variational NN inference, Martens & Grosse KFAC, prior MLE/Fisher selection like Zhang & Oles 2000 and submodular/batch works such as Wei et al. 2015), so the main novelty is their non‑trivial integration and engineering to scale to CNNs rather than a wholly new theoretical approach. Thus the contribution is a useful, but largely compositional, advancement (moderate novelty). | B(3): The paper combines known ingredients—variational free energy / Bayesian/MLE asymptotics (Sec.3), Graves-style variational approximation (Related Work), and Kronecker-factored Fisher (K-FAC) approximations (Sec.3.2 referencing Martens & Grosse)—to produce a scalable batch active-learning criterion and a greedy selection procedure (Sec.4). While the specific assembly (backprop-free criterion using MLE-based Gaussian posteriors + K-FAC for CNNs) appears practically useful and is presented as a first application of these pieces to batch active learning for deep nets, it is largely an integration and engineering of existing techniques rather than a fundamentally new theory or algorithmic paradigm (cf. Zhang & Oles (2000) and Graves (2011) connections noted in the paper). Thus the contribution is moderately novel (incremental but non-trivial).

reviewer_a.justification: The paper brings a moderately novel combination: it applies variational free‑energy/MLE asymptotic Gaussian posterior ideas (Sec.3) to pool‑based batch active learning for deep CNNs and makes the approach scalable via Kronecker‑factored (KFAC) approximations of the Fisher (Sec.3.2) and further heuristic approximations to obtain a greedy selection criterion. However the individual ingredients are established (Graves 2011 variational NN inference, Martens & Grosse KFAC, prior MLE/Fisher selection like Zhang & Oles 2000 and submodular/batch works such as Wei et al. 2015), so the main novelty is their non‑trivial integration and engineering to scale to CNNs rather than a wholly new theoretical approach. Thus the contribution is a useful, but largely compositional, advancement (moderate novelty).

reviewer_b.justification: The paper combines known ingredients—variational free energy / Bayesian/MLE asymptotics (Sec.3), Graves-style variational approximation (Related Work), and Kronecker-factored Fisher (K-FAC) approximations (Sec.3.2 referencing Martens & Grosse)—to produce a scalable batch active-learning criterion and a greedy selection procedure (Sec.4). While the specific assembly (backprop-free criterion using MLE-based Gaussian posteriors + K-FAC for CNNs) appears practically useful and is presented as a first application of these pieces to batch active learning for deep nets, it is largely an integration and engineering of existing techniques rather than a fundamentally new theory or algorithmic paradigm (cf. Zhang & Oles (2000) and Graves (2011) connections noted in the paper). Thus the contribution is moderately novel (incremental but non-trivial).

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): Overall structure is logical (intro → related work → method → experiments → conclusion) and the main contributions are stated early (batch active learning for deep nets via a variational/Fisher/KFAC criterion). However presentation suffers from clarity issues: figures/tables and some algorithm pseudocode are referenced but missing, and the author/title/header is garbled. Notation is introduced but occasionally confusing (many W*, WX, WY variants and I_A/I_X usage that are not always clearly explained) and the text contains repeated sentences, grammatical errors and typos (e.g. “proportionnal”, “its has”, duplicated paragraphs near the end), which make some derivations hard to follow. | B(3): The paper is broadly well-organized (Intro → Related Work → Method/Approximations → Greedy algorithm → Experiments → Discussion/Conclusion), so the overall flow is logical. However presentation suffers from missing and referenced-but-unshown elements (e.g. Figure 1/2/3, Table 1/2 and pseudocode are cited but not present), a cluttered header/title area, and duplicated/fragmented text in the final pages. Notation and exposition are adequate in places but inconsistent or under-explained (many Fisher/trace approximations use symbols like ∝∼ or shorthand IS/IA/IY without always re-defining them clearly), and the writing contains numerous grammatical errors and typos (e.g. "proportionnal", "objetif"), broken citations and repeated sentences, which make some sections hard to follow.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall structure is logical (intro → related work → method → experiments → conclusion) and the main contributions are stated early (batch active learning for deep nets via a variational/Fisher/KFAC criterion). However presentation suffers from clarity issues: figures/tables and some algorithm pseudocode are referenced but missing, and the author/title/header is garbled. Notation is introduced but occasionally confusing (many W*, WX, WY variants and I_A/I_X usage that are not always clearly explained) and the text contains repeated sentences, grammatical errors and typos (e.g. “proportionnal”, “its has”, duplicated paragraphs near the end), which make some derivations hard to follow.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is broadly well-organized (Intro → Related Work → Method/Approximations → Greedy algorithm → Experiments → Discussion/Conclusion), so the overall flow is logical. However presentation suffers from missing and referenced-but-unshown elements (e.g. Figure 1/2/3, Table 1/2 and pseudocode are cited but not present), a cluttered header/title area, and duplicated/fragmented text in the final pages. Notation and exposition are adequate in places but inconsistent or under-explained (many Fisher/trace approximations use symbols like ∝∼ or shorthand IS/IA/IY without always re-defining them clearly), and the writing contains numerous grammatical errors and typos (e.g. "proportionnal", "objetif"), broken citations and repeated sentences, which make some sections hard to follow.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=2] A(3): The paper proposes a plausible variational/Fisher-based batch selection framework but relies on multiple unjustified or mathematically weak approximations (e.g. replacing traces of matrix inverses by inverses of traces, bounding determinants by trace-based functions, and the mysterious N√π factor in Eq.12–13) that are neither derived nor motivated rigorously. It also assumes the current MLE ŵ approximates both W*X and W*Y and uses predicted labels for unlabeled Fisher estimates—strong assumptions that are especially dubious early in active learning (Sec.3.3–3.4). The experimental evaluation is limited (only MNIST/USPS), lacks stronger modern baselines (e.g. BALD/BADGE/core-set), and reports only averaged runs without statistical tests or detailed ablations of the key approximations. Overall the idea is interesting but the mathematical derivations are incomplete and key assumptions/approximations and empirical validation are insufficiently supported. | B(2): The paper contains significant technical issues that undermine its main derivation: the second-order expansion (Eq.10–11) incorrectly replaces the Hessian with an inverse Fisher (the quadratic term should involve the Hessian/Fisher itself, not its inverse), and several subsequent manipulations use very loose/unjustified bounds (e.g. approximating trace(inv) by inv(trace) and determinant by trace) that break mathematical correctness of the criterion. The KL/variational approximations (Eq.14–18) compound these approximations, so the resulting selection criterion is not rigorously derived. Empirically the experiments (Sec.5) are limited (only MNIST/USPS, weak baselines, no statistical tests or stronger active-learning baselines), so they do not compensate for the theoretical gaps.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a plausible variational/Fisher-based batch selection framework but relies on multiple unjustified or mathematically weak approximations (e.g. replacing traces of matrix inverses by inverses of traces, bounding determinants by trace-based functions, and the mysterious N√π factor in Eq.12–13) that are neither derived nor motivated rigorously. It also assumes the current MLE ŵ approximates both W*X and W*Y and uses predicted labels for unlabeled Fisher estimates—strong assumptions that are especially dubious early in active learning (Sec.3.3–3.4). The experimental evaluation is limited (only MNIST/USPS), lacks stronger modern baselines (e.g. BALD/BADGE/core-set), and reports only averaged runs without statistical tests or detailed ablations of the key approximations. Overall the idea is interesting but the mathematical derivations are incomplete and key assumptions/approximations and empirical validation are insufficiently supported.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper contains significant technical issues that undermine its main derivation: the second-order expansion (Eq.10–11) incorrectly replaces the Hessian with an inverse Fisher (the quadratic term should involve the Hessian/Fisher itself, not its inverse), and several subsequent manipulations use very loose/unjustified bounds (e.g. approximating trace(inv) by inv(trace) and determinant by trace) that break mathematical correctness of the criterion. The KL/variational approximations (Eq.14–18) compound these approximations, so the resulting selection criterion is not rigorously derived. Empirically the experiments (Sec.5) are limited (only MNIST/USPS, weak baselines, no statistical tests or stronger active-learning baselines), so they do not compensate for the theoretical gaps.
